explanation.text: The Egyptians arrive at a desert along the Nile River's banks and step off their boats onto land. In this setting, “banks” means the sloping pieces of land beside the river.
The workers are cutting, pulling, and placing giant stones to build Khufu's tomb, which the article calls their most important work. Therefore, the mighty “task” is a job that needs to be done.
The section explains that Khufu wanted more than a strong tomb: he ordered the tallest tomb ever built, reaching 481 feet. That decision is the section's main point.
Paragraph 6 names rooms, hallways, and air shafts needed inside the pyramid. The cutaway diagram places and links all those parts, so readers can understand how the pyramid's interior is connected.

The statement that workers came to Giza “from every part of Egypt” directly identifies both their destination and their many places of origin. It therefore best supports the idea of nationwide participation.

The article explains why Khufu ordered the Great Pyramid and how thousands of Egyptians planned, cut, transported, and placed its stones. These details support the main idea that building the king's tomb was an important national job.
Although Abigail badly wants the kitten, she stays quiet because her brother says appearing calm can work better when asking for something. Thus, “play it cool” means not seeming too eager.

Spot crawls onto Abigail's chest, nestles into her neck, and becomes completely still, establishing their immediate bond. Therefore, that central connection is more important than shopping, popcorn, or the rescue worker's book.
Mother's suggestion that they ask Dad, along with “maybe it's time,” shows that she is reconsidering the old refusal and may support getting a cat now that the children are older.

The rescue worker lets Abigail hold Spot after warning her to be gentle, then points out that the kitten likes her and does not try to escape. Those observations suggest she believes Abigail could be a good owner.
Abigail promises to feed Spot, change his litter box, and spend her own allowance on toys. Her willingness to accept new chores and costs shows how caring for a pet can encourage responsibility.

Changed weather in the Cocoa Belt causes cacao trees to grow fewer pods and beans of lower quality while pests and disease spread. These effects therefore develop the central idea that climate change is reducing chocolate production.

Weather stations provide current information that tells farmers when to plant and fertilize, replacing old seasonal rules that no longer always work. They therefore guide the planting and care of cacao trees.
The proposed fixes could produce more cacao on less land, protect rainforests, preserve chocolate, and benefit the planet. Because all those outcomes are positive, “a sweet deal” means a good result.

The numbered section follows chocolate production in order: growing and picking pods, removing, fermenting, and drying beans, then roasting and blending them into candy. Therefore, it presents a step-by-step process.
The photograph shows an opened cacao pod with individual beans visible inside it. That image directly illustrates the nearby step explaining how farmers separate the beans from their pods.
Spending more than $22 billion on chocolate in a single year demonstrates enormous demand for the treat. That amount is strong evidence that many Americans enjoy eating chocolate.
